label: vulnerable

source.description: User-controlled URL string bound from the HTTP query parameter via @RequestParam in the controller layer. Spring performs no sanitization during binding.

propagation.description: url is forwarded unchanged to ProxyService.fetchRemote() — no validation at the controller-to-service boundary.
ProxyService.fetchRemote() passes url directly to HttpUtil.getUrl() without any sanitization — no validation at the service-to-util boundary.

sink.description: RestTemplate.getForObject() initiates an outbound HTTP request to the attacker-controlled URL. Internal addresses such as http://169.254.169.254/ are reachable.

expected_findings.risk_reasoning: The url value propagates through three layers (controller → service → util) without any validation. It enters RestTemplate.getForObject() in the util layer. An attacker can target cloud metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254), internal services, or arbitrary internet hosts.
expected_findings.remediation_hint: Validate the URL in the service layer before delegating to util: parse with java.net.URL, enforce https-only scheme, restrict hostnames to a domain allowlist, and block private/loopback/link-local IP ranges. Configure RestTemplate with setInstanceFollowRedirects(false).

recall_anchor.description: A correct system must identify the sink in util/HttpUtil.java. Source identification in controller/ProxyController.java is additional credit.

notes: Cross-file SSRF taint tracing: controller (source) → service (passthrough) → util (sink). Each layer boundary is an opportunity for a system to lose track of the taint.
